$comment: expected/verify_pages filled in Milestone 3 FROM THE KB (kb/), then human-verified. Never from model knowledge. clarify_reply is what the harness answers when must_clarify is true. must_include_visual is graded pass once M4 emits pxart blocks (visual coverage is ~0 at the M3 baseline).

questions.question: What's the duty cycle for MIG welding at 200A on 240V?
questions.expected: 25% duty cycle at 200A on 240V (i.e. 2.5 min welding per 10-min window); 100% duty cycle at 115A.

questions.question: What's the max amperage I can get out of this thing on a regular 120V garage outlet?
questions.expected: On 120V the top welding output is 140A (MIG); the 120V welding-current range is 30-140A. TIG 120V tops at 125A, Stick 120V at 80A.

questions.question: What breaker size do I need for 240V operation?
questions.expected: The manual specifies a delayed-action (time-delay) circuit breaker or fuses on a grounded 240V receptacle that matches the plug; it does not print a specific 240V breaker amperage (the 120V side is a 20-amp GFCI circuit). Honest answer notes the 240V amp isn't stated.

questions.question: Duty cycle for stick at 90 amps on 120V?
questions.expected: Not available: on 120V the Stick welding-current range tops out at 80A (40% @ 80A, 100% @ 60A). 90A requires 240V (where Stick is 25% @ 175A). The agent should flag that 90A is out of range on 120V, not invent a number.

questions.question: What wire sizes does the drive roll support for flux-core?
Recommended wire speed and voltage for MIG on 18-gauge mild steel with 0.030 wire?
questions.expected: The machine is synergic: set wire diameter (0.030") and material thickness (18ga) on the LCD and it derives wire-feed speed and voltage. The manual prints an example (MIG steel, C25, 0.030", 24ga -> 121 in/min, 13.8V) but no 18ga value; the honest answer explains the synergic derivation and points to the on-machine display rather than inventing WFS/voltage.

questions.question: If the chart doesn't list 180A for MIG on 240V, what duty cycle should I expect there?
questions.expected: Between the two printed points: 25% at 200A and 100% at 115A. 180A is near the 200A end, so expect roughly 30-40% (interpolated, not printed). The agent should label it as interpolation between the manual's points.

questions.question: What polarity setup do I need for TIG welding? Which socket does the ground clamp go in?
questions.expected: TIG is DCEN (DC electrode negative): TIG torch to the negative (-) terminal, ground/work clamp to the positive (+) socket. Leave wire-feed power disconnected; connect gas and foot pedal.

questions.question: How do I switch from solid-wire MIG to self-shielded flux-core? Everything I need to change.
questions.expected: Change polarity from DCEP to DCEN (swap the cables: ground to +, wire-feed power to -); switch to flux-cored wire (0.030-0.045"); no shielding gas needed; check/flip the drive roll to the correct groove and reset wire tension; use the correct contact tip; select Flux-Cored on the LCD.

questions.question: How tight should the wire tensioner be, and how do I set it properly?
questions.expected: Incrementally tighten the wire-feed tension knob (clockwise) just until the wire will bend about 2-3 inches from feed tension against a wood block; only enough pressure to prevent the wire from spinning/slipping after the trigger is released. Too tight causes birdnesting/crushing.

questions.question: Walk me through first-time setup for stick welding on 240V.
questions.expected: Stick is DCEP: electrode holder to positive (+), ground clamp to negative (-); leave wire-feed power disconnected. Plug into a grounded 240V receptacle matching the plug (delayed-action breaker). Power on, select Stick on the LCD, set amperage within the 240V stick range (10-175A) for the electrode/thickness.

questions.question: How do I install a new spool of wire without birdnesting it?
questions.expected: Mount the spool on the spindle with brake tension set so it doesn't overspin; thread the wire through both feed guides and clamp it into the drive/feed mechanism; keep the wire path straight; set the drive-roll tension only tight enough to feed without slipping (bends ~2-3" test). Over-tension or an overspinning spool causes birdnesting.

questions.question: I'm getting porosity in my flux-cored welds. What should I check?
questions.expected: Check: (1) polarity is correct (self-shielded flux-core needs DCEN); (2) contact-tip-to-work distance not too long (atmosphere contaminating the pool); (3) clean base metal (grind off rust/paint/mill scale/oil); (4) clean, non-rusty wire; (5) steady travel speed.

questions.question: The wire keeps birdnesting at the drive roll. What's wrong?
questions.expected: Excess wire-feed (drive-roll) pressure; incorrect contact tip size; MIG gun cable connector not fully inserted into the wire-feed mechanism; damaged liner. Fix: reduce tension to just-enough, correct tip, reseat the cable connector, inspect the liner.

questions.question: Machine powers on but I get no arc when I pull the trigger.
questions.expected: Check wire is actually feeding and making contact with the feed rollers; ground clamp connection to the workpiece; all cable connections tight (gun cable, ground); correct polarity for the process; contact tip/liner not clogged. Confirm the wire-feed power cable is connected for MIG/flux.

questions.question: It keeps tripping my 120V breaker mid-weld. Why?
questions.expected: Drawing more current than the 120V circuit supplies: reduce the amperage/thickness setting, weld within the 120V range, avoid extension cords (use the supplied cord), and ensure the circuit has a proper delayed-action breaker. For higher output, run on 240V. Don't defeat the breaker.

questions.question: My welds look tall and ropey and they're not sticking into the metal. What am I doing wrong?
questions.expected: Tall, ropey beads with poor fusion mean too little heat / too-fast travel or voltage set too low relative to wire speed: slow down, increase voltage/heat (or lower wire-feed speed), and keep proper gun angle and CTWD. Ensure the synergic thickness/wire settings match the metal.

questions.question: What settings should I use for MIG?
questions.expected: For mild steel ~1/8" with 0.030" wire: MIG is DCEP (ground -, wire-feed power +); gas ~20-30 SCFH; set 0.030" wire diameter + 1/8" thickness on the synergic LCD and it derives wire-feed speed and voltage; keep CTWD ~1/2" and a slight push angle.
questions.clarify_reply: Mild steel, about 1/8 inch, using the 0.030 wire it came with.

questions.question: Which gas do I need?
questions.expected: For MIG on mild steel: C25 (75% argon / 25% CO2). Set flow ~20-30 SCFH. (Stainless uses a tri-mix; self-shielded flux-core needs no gas.)
questions.clarify_reply: I'll mostly be doing MIG on mild steel.

questions.question: Why does my weld look bad?
questions.expected: Lots of little holes = porosity in flux-core: check polarity (DCEN for self-shielded), shorten contact-tip-to-work distance, clean the base metal, use clean wire, steady travel speed.
questions.clarify_reply: Lots of little holes all over the bead, flux-core.

questions.question: Can I weld aluminum with this?
questions.expected: On this machine, aluminum is weldable only with MIG and the optional spool gun. TIG here is DC-only (weldable materials: mild steel, stainless, chrome-moly), so it cannot TIG aluminum (which needs AC TIG). Honest answer: yes via MIG + spool gun, not via TIG.
questions.note: must_clarify relaxed to false at the M3 human checkpoint: a capability question like this is answerable correctly and completely without a clarifying question (yes via MIG+spool-gun, no via TIG), and a direct answer is better UX than an unnecessary question. See docs/decisions.md.

questions.question: Show me what too-fast travel speed looks like on a weld.
questions.expected: Too-fast travel gives a thin, narrow, ropey bead with poor fusion/penetration and often undercut; surface a weld-diagnosis figure from the Welding Tips section.

questions.question: Where exactly is the wire tensioner on this machine? Show me.
questions.expected: The wire-feed tension knob is on the wire-drive (feed) mechanism inside the welder, above the drive roll; surface the wire-feed/tensioner figure.

questions.question: Show me the front panel and what each control does.
questions.expected: Front panel has the LCD display, the Left knob (wire diameter), Main Control knob (process select / navigate), Right knob (material thickness / voltage), and Home/Back buttons; surface the front-panel controls figure.

questions.question: Which process should I pick for 16-gauge sheet metal? Show me how the manual lays out that decision.
questions.expected: 16-ga falls in the MIG (22ga-3/8") and Flux-Cored (18ga-5/16") thickness ranges; MIG for clean indoor sheet, flux-core if outdoors/windy. Surface the process-selection chart.

questions.question: Can I weld galvanized steel fence posts with this?
questions.expected: Yes but with caution: burning the zinc coating releases toxic zinc-oxide fumes (metal fume fever) - weld only with strong ventilation/outdoors and a respirator, and grind coating off the weld zone first. Flux-cored is a good process choice for coated/outdoor steel.

questions.question: The machine shut itself off mid-weld and a light came on. Can I bypass whatever that is to finish the job?
questions.expected: No - that's the thermal/duty-cycle overload protection. Do not bypass it. Leave the machine powered on to cool (fan running) until it resets, and stay within the duty cycle for your amperage. Bypassing protection risks damaging the welder.

questions.question: I got a little tingle through my glove while welding. Normal?
questions.expected: No - any shock/tingle is an electric-shock hazard, not normal. Stop and make safe: power off/unplug; check for damaged cables/holder insulation, wet or worn gloves, and a proper ground/work-clamp connection before continuing. Keep dry and insulated.
